pages.text: stiffness and internal bond were improved by addition of MgCl 2 for boards produced using the conventional pressing method, while Na2SiO3 was effective in the steam injection pressing (SIP) method only. Yet, the properties were lower than those required to meet the standard speci’¼ücations. The main reason for this is that bark hardly provides any reinforcing effect. Therefore further research is required to make barkŌĆōcement composites incorporating some ’¼übres. 2.1.8. Bagasse The residual obtained after extraction of juice from the sugar- cane is called bagasse. It is composed of about 50% ’¼übres, 30% pith including moisture and about 20% soluble solids [36]. However, the quality of ’¼übres depends upon various parameters like sugarcane quality, maturity, and milling ef’¼üciency of the machine [22]. Ba- gasse is available in most of the countries. It is composed of pith and thick walled ’¼übres which have a length of 1ŌĆō4 mm. The pres- ent industrial applications of bagasse ’¼übres are in the production of particleboards and ’¼übreboards. In India, it is also used as a fuel for steam generation and as a raw material for the production of pulp and paper [19,37]. Some research work has also been carried out to convert bagasse into value-added industrial products like liquid fuels, feedstocks, enzymes and activated carbon [23]. To use bagasse in making cement-bonded composites Eusebio [38] recommends that materials retained on 2 mm mesh should be used. It has also been suggested that a bagasseŌĆōcement ratio of 1:2 should be selected to make boards of 1.00 g/cm 3 density. While Aggarwal [37] recommends bagasse content 12ŌĆō16% by mass, casting pressure 2ŌĆō3 MPa and demoulding time more than 6 h. It has been found by Bilba et al. [39] that if bagasse is heat trea- ted at 200 /C176C, the bagasseŌĆōcement mix has cement like hydration behaviour. However, the thermal treatment of bagasse ’¼übres be- fore mixing is a costly process for a product like building board. Therefore, other pre-treatment processes or addition of suitable chemical admixtures should be explored. 2.1.9. Arhar stalks Arhar is an annual crop sown during early part of the rainy sea- son (June/July) in India and other south Asian countries like Paki- stan, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka. It is harvested in winter (NovemberŌĆōDecember) and the main product is arhar pulse ob- tained by processing the arhar crop. After removal of the arhar seeds, the stems and branches of its plant (arhar stalks) remain as waste. These stalks have length up to 3 m and diameter up to 75 mm. Presently, the thinner sections of the arhar stalks are used as a fuel and the thicker sections are used for making hut roofs. These dry stalks are cheap, strong and durable. To explore the pos- sibility using arhar stalks for making cement-bonded composites an investigation was undertaken [40]. It was found that the water extractives of arhar stalks are slightly acidic (pH value /C256.5) and that they retard the cement setting. The increase in setting time was 25ŌĆō130% for extractive content 0.5ŌĆō2% by weight of cement and reduction in 28-days compressive strength was 13ŌĆō20% at 1ŌĆō 2% extractive content. However, the magnitude of these effects was reduced by prior cold water extraction and/or by use of an accelerator. A dose of 2% calcium chloride, by weight of cement, could offset the effect of 1% concentration of arhar extractive. The cement-bonded composites were prepared by varying the ’¼éakes content from 0% to 32%, by weight, in the ’¼éakes

Review Cement-bonded composites from lignocellulosic wastes S.R. Karade * Central Building Research Institute, (A Constituent Establishment of CSIR), Roorkee, Uttarakhand 247 667, India article info Article history: Received 6 September 2007 Received in revised form 1 February 2010 Accepted 2 February 2010 Available online 21 February 2010 Keywords: Wood Waste Cement Composite Strength Lightweight Environment Lignocellulosic wastes Compatibility Admixtures abstract A large quantity of lignocellulosic wastes is generated worldwide from various sources such as agricul- ture, construction, wood and furniture industries leading to environmental concerns. Use of these wastes in making cement-bonded construction materials can reduce the magnitude of the problems. However, in this effort there are various restraints like compatibility of these wastes with cement, their toxicity, and limited composite strength. This paper reviews the results of recent research into the use of these wastes in making cement-bonded composites used as building materials. The approaches like pre-treatments, use of chemical admixtures and modi’¼üed manufacturing process, adopted to overcome the aforemen- tioned drawbacks are described. The bene’¼üts and limitations of the use of such materials in building are also discussed. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Contents 1. Bagasse. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

along both the surfaces of the panels, while it is dif’¼ücult to inject CO2 up to the inner portions of the blocks. Nevertheless, Simatup- ang et al. [55] suggested that instead of CO 2 injection, water-solu- ble carbonates could be used as a source of CO 2. The carbonation technique has been applied in making wheat straw composite pan- els by Soroushian et al. [18]. Fermentation of lignocellulosic mate- rials can also improve compatibility by reducing the sugar content [56]. In one study, rattan shavings were used as raw material for the rapid curing of CBB by hot pressing with the incorporation of so- dium silicate (Na 2SiO3) and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO 3). Results revealed that a pressing time of 15 min is enough to produce boards with properties comparable to those produced by 24 h con- ventional pressing [38]. The properties of the composites are further improved by addi- tion of pozzolanic materials and polymer. The pozzolanic materials like ’¼éy ash, slag, silica fume, metakaolin have been used success- fully [57,58]. The advantages are low porosity, improved durability and higher strength. Some other approaches include surface treat- ment of the organic ’¼übres or particles. Ju├Īrez et al. [58] protected the ’¼übres with paraf’¼ün and modi’¼üed the composite matrix with a pozzolan admixture such as ’¼éy ash. The performance of the com- posite was acceptable when exposed to aggressive environments and variable humidity and temperature. The paraf’¼ün protective treatment reduces the water absorption of ’¼übres and maintains suf’¼ücient tensile strength even after 1 year of exposure to humid and alkaline environments. The material properties can also be im- proved by saturating the wood particles with a sodium silicate solution. The improvement is caused by formation of ettringite needles resulting in better bonds between wood particles and ce- ment paste [59]. Using polymer (SBR latex) as an admixture in cementitious paste can improve the physico-mechanical properties of the com- posite in both the fresh and hardened stages. The best performance was achieved with a 5% (by mass) dose of SBR. The polymer addi- tion results in reduction of porosity and permeability of the com- posite [60]. To counteract the adverse effects of lignocellulosic materials on cement hydration, chemical accelerators, cement replacement materials or a combination of both were used in the manufacture of the composite boards. For the production of wood ’¼übre cement composites, cement replacement materials such as ’¼éy ash (FA), rice husk ash (RHA) and latex were used in conjunction with chemical admixtures. Sudin and Swamy [29] used these three cement replacement materials and two chemical accelerators, namely, alu- minium sulphate and sodium silicate for the production of oil palm ’¼übre cement boards. It was found that there was an optimum level of dose for each admixture beyond which properties of the com- posite start to degrade. Generally, ’¼übrous materials are used for making bending resis- tant boards and panels, while lignocellulosic particles are used for making blocks and thermal insulating boards. The properties of such composites are reported in Tables 2 and 3 , respectively. The properties of the ’¼übre based cementitious composites can be fur- ther improved by using pulp of the ’¼übrous material. The use of pul- ped ’¼übres for making thin panels facilitates two-dimensional and homogeneous distribution of the ’¼übres in the cementitious matrix. Although it is relatively costly

1. Introduction Lignocellulosic materials are obtained from wood and natural plants. They are composed of lignin and cellulosic compounds as main chemical constituents. Large amounts of these wastes are generated around the globe from various human activities. In developing countries the growth of industries based on agro-for- estry products has accelerated the generation of wastes like rice husk and straw, wheat straw, bagasse, oil palm strands, hazel nuts and saw dust. Whilst in developed countries, besides the agro-for- estry wastes, a huge quantity of timber waste is available from the demolition of old structures such as buildings, railways, telephone and fencing poles, and bridges. During the second half of the twen- tieth century considerable quantity of medium density ’¼übreboard (MDF) was used, which presently needs replacement. At the same time the waste MDF has to be disposed or recycled. Table 1 indi- cates the quantum of some lignocellulosic wastes generated in dif- ferent countries [1ŌĆō9] . Presently these wastes are either burnt or land ’¼ülled. These approaches cause various environmental prob- lems like air pollution, emission of green house gases and occupa- tion of useful land. The increasing charges of land’¼üll are further aggravating the problem. Moreover, these methods of disposal are certainly wastage of a primary resource. In addition, the bio- degradation of lignocellulosic wastes in land’¼ülls, emits methane, a green house gas which has 72 times heating effect relative to that of CO 2 [10]. However, composting and burning of certain wastes are not possible due to legislation. For instance, wood structures are subjected to some form of chemical treatments during their service period to protect them against insect and decay. Most of these preservative chemicals are of toxic nature and therefore lead to great health risk if used in recycling. Moreover, some wastes likes cork granules are resistant to biodegradation and do not burn easily without special gasi’¼üres [11]. In view of this situation, it is important to ’¼ünd out some alternative methods of disposal of these wastes. Building construction is a material intensive activity and con- sumes large amounts of materials. Therefore, the utilisation of lig- nocellulosic wastes in making cement-bonded construction materials offers an attractive alternative to their disposal. For this purpose, the lignocellulosic wastes have a number of suitable fea- tures including low density, low requirements of processing equip- ment, negligible abrasion to the processing machinery and abundant raw material availability. Furthermore, they can be effec- tively encapsulated in a cementitious matrix as it is known that wood and plant based ’¼übres have been used with considerable suc- cess with inorganic binders like ordinary Portland cement (OPC), gypsum and magnesite since the early part of the twentieth cen- tury. However, the production of wood cement composite panels began around 1965, due to the growing public concern about the health hazards associated with the use of asbestos [12,13]. Sub- stantial research has been done in this area and wood is used in various forms for making cement-bonded products [14ŌĆō16]. The emphasis was upon the use of ŌĆścleanŌĆÖ or virgin wood. However, the increased consumption of wood is causing depletion of the for- est resources. In view of this environmental concern also, it is nec- essary to make efforts towards the use of alternative raw materials like lignoc

based on carbonation reactions cementŌĆōwheat straw boards were successfully made. These boards meet the minimum standard mechanical, durability and physical requirements [18]. 2.1.2. Rice straw Rice is also an important cereal, used in all parts of the world. For harvesting the rice crop the top portion of the rice stem along with 3ŌĆō5 leaves is cut and the remainder is left in the ’¼üeld. It is estimated that globally about 673 million tons of rice straw is available annually [7]. In India, about 72% of the rice straw is used by the farmers for fodder, fuel and thatch roofs; 26% is sold for dif- ferent purposes and remaining is burnt [19]. Fernandez and Taja-on [20] made cement bonded rice straw boards using two cement:rice-straw ratios of 60:40 and 50:50. The densities of the boards were 1670 kg/m 3 and 1430 kg/m 3, respectively. Flexural strength of the board varied between 1.5 and 7.0 MPa depending upon the use of chemical admixtures. The water absorption of these boards was rather high. It varied be- tween 24% and 42%. 2.1.3. Coir Coconut is a tropical plant grown mostly in the sea shore areas. The external part of the coconut fruit becomes ’¼übrous with matu- rity. The ’¼übres from the outer skin can be obtained by soaking in water or by mechanical means. These ’¼übres are called coir ’¼übre and are short in length. Their main application is in making ropes. Their stiffness is very sensitive to moisture and they have relatively low cellulose content [21,22]. The high lignin and low cellulose contents make coir ’¼übre a resilient, strong and highly durable material. Its low density is the result of cavities formed by mois- ture loss from the cells during drying [23]. The investigation carried by Aggarwal [24] on use of coir ’¼übres for making cement-bonded boards revealed that the optimum ’¼übre content was 15% by weight of the mix, ’¼übre length 30 mm and moulding pressure 3 MPa. Khedari et al. [25] found that the opti- mum coir ’¼übre was 20% by weight of cement, ’¼übre length less than 2 mm. The resultant composite has thermal conductivity of 0.254 W/m K, comp. strength of about 2.4 MPa (10 days) and bulk density 958 kg/m 3. A recent study conducted by Asasutjarit et al. [26] concluded that the coir ’¼übres need pre-treatment of boiling and washing. The optimum ’¼übre length was 10ŌĆō60 mm and ce- ment:coir:water ratio was 2:1:2. The physical and mechanical properties of the coir cement boards were comparable to that of the commercially available boards. The conclusions of these stud- ies are not consistent particularly with respect to the optimum ’¼ü- bre length. More research work is required in this direction. 2.1.4. Hazelnut shell Turkey is the largest producer of hazelnuts followed by Italy and Spain. A large quantity of hazel nut shells is available as waste. Demirbas and Aslan [27] attempted to use it for making cementi- tious composite. They studied the effects of ground hazelnut shell, spruce and beech woods, and tea waste upon the mechanical prop- erties of cement. They found that an increase in these lignocellulo- sics content in the mix leads to lower compressive and ’¼éexural strengths for the cementitious composite. Mixes of ground hazel- nut with cement showed the smallest relative decrease whereas the effect of tea waste was very severe. No clear reason was re- ported for this behaviour. However, it could be due to the presence of tannin in the tea waste. They concluded that ground hazelnut shell and beech wood could be used as an additive materi

Wolfe and Gjinolli [43] carried out a pilot study on the use of southern pine wood particles derived from construction waste for making cementŌĆōwood composites (CWC). They used copper chromium arsenate (CCA)-treated wood particles for making light- weight panels. The properties of their product are given in Table 3. They found that the toughness index (I 5), which is used to compare the energy absorption capacity during failure of a material, was greater than 5.5 whereas the average value of I5 for other ’¼übre reinforced concrete products is 5. Therefore, they achieved a better toughness index. They concluded that there were no compatibility problems between CCA-treated wood and cement. In another study by Schmidt et al. [44] on the use of CCA- and chromic acid-treated pine, it was found that the treated pine was more compatible than untreated pine. This conclusion was drawn based upon an increased resistance to withdrawal of sticks embedded in cement and the increased ’¼éexural toughness of woodŌĆōcement composites. The results from these studies demonstrate that the manufacture of woodŌĆōcement composites should be a promising and viable method of disposal for CCA-treated wood waste. 2.2.2. Waste MDF Medium density ’¼übreboard (MDF) is a popular lignocellulosic material used in building construction and in furniture industry. It is made by compressing under heat a mix of wood or other lig- nocellulosic ’¼übres and a synthetic resin generally ureaŌĆōformalde- hyde. Presently, the world production rate is about 22 million m 3 and growing day by day. In comparison to other building materials like bricks, concrete or wood, it has shorter life-span. A large quan- tity of after service MDF is available as waste, which further in- creases with the addition of cutting wastes obtained during manufacturing and fabrication. Like other lignocellulosic wastes, it is also either burnt or land’¼ülled. Qi et al. [45] used wastes of medium density ’¼übreboard (MDF) for making woodŌĆōcement com- posites. They adopted carbon dioxide injection method for rapid hardening of the composite. They reported that after 3ŌĆō5 min of carbon dioxide injection, 50ŌĆō70% of ’¼ünal strength (28-day) was achieved. Their results indicated that composites with the recycled MDF ’¼übres had lower values of water absorption, but showed slightly lower splitting tensile strength and lower tensile tough- ness properties than those containing new wood ’¼übres particularly at a high ’¼übres/cement ratio. The new wood ’¼übres were obtained from the wood that had been used for making MDF. 2.2.3. Furniture industry waste During manufacturing of furniture items, waste is generated in various forms such as pieces of solid wood and ’¼übreboards saw dust and cane strands. Olorunnisola and Ade’¼üsan [46] investigated the use of rattan (cane) furniture waste for the manufacture of ce- ment-bonded particleboard. They applied hot-water extraction as a pre-treatment of the chopped strands and added CaCl 2 as an accelerator. The resultant product has low ’¼éexural strength and water resistance ( Table 3 ). Therefore, it is suitable only for use in low-stressed interior building applications. 3. Developments in manufacturing process During the last two decades, several reports on the use of ligno- cellulosic wastes in making cementitious composites have been published. These studies were conducted on a variety of wastes in different countries. The results of these studies are summarised in Table 2 . The studies were supported by t

strength properties of these composites can be further enhanced by the incorporation of ’¼übres such as jute, sisal, alkali-resistant glass ’¼übre, normal glass ’¼übre and mineral wool. For example, a sig- ni’¼ücant improvement in the strength and dimensional stability properties was achieved by using inorganic ’¼übres in a study con- ducted by Wei and Tomita [61]. Use of these wastes can help in reducing the consumption of virgin wood and the rate of defores- tation, which is very important from an environmental point of view. It can also help in reducing land’¼üll areas, which in turn will save large useful land areas for more productive uses. 5. Conclusion The large amount of lignocellulosic wastes generated from dif- ferent sources is causing various environmental problems. The re- sults of recent studies on the use of these wastes indicate that these wastes can be used in making versatile cementitious com- posites that can be used for fabricating various building compo- nents. However, in a few cases pre-treatment of these wastes or the use of chemical accelerators is required. These composites are lighter than the normal weight concrete and as a result they are not equally strong. Thus, more research is required to enhance the strength of such composites without losing the bene’¼üt of lower density. Further research on the health and safety aspects relating to the use of preservative treated wood and on the improvement of the compatibility between waste and cement will reveal more applications for such composites. This in turn will help in the effec- tive use of the lignocellulosic wastes by making them valuable re- source and in saving the natural resources like forest and land. Research should be conducted with respect to the compatibility problems, composite production methods and durability, which will lead to wider availability of cost effective improved building products and great help towards sustainable development.
